# Questions in Livestream 46

Live Stream #46: God Is Our Refuge And Strength.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12</a> | Are demons fallen angels, dead Nephilim, or something else? | He leans toward identifying demons as fallen angels rather than the spirits of dead Nephilim. | He says he would classify demons especially as fallen angels, while rejecting Michael Heiser's idea that the Nephilim are their own special class of spirits. He treats "demon" more like a broad job description for spirits with agency, so some demonic beings could also include regional powers or other spirits acting within the forces of darkness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:52</a> | How would ancient Egyptians have understood `elohim`, and how does that differ from modern usage? | He says `elohim` is Hebrew, not Egyptian; the Egyptian category for gods or spirits was broader and would cover many kinds of divine beings. | He begins by correcting the category: `elohim` is Hebrew, while Egyptian would use words such as `netjer` for a god. In Egyptian usage that category was extremely broad, covering major gods, lesser spirits, and malevolent spirits without the later monotheistic distinction modern readers often assume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:25</a> | Do ANE texts that praise a god as incomparable undermine biblical monotheistic proof texts? | He says no. Similar rhetoric elsewhere is hyperbolic, but biblical monotheistic claims are more explicit and programmatic. | He says Egyptian texts like Sinuhe use courtly or devotional hyperbole to magnify a particular god, not to assert philosophical monotheism. By contrast, he points to Isaiah's explicit claims that Yahweh is first and last and that there is no other god, while also distinguishing later prophetic monotheism from the Pentateuch's more practical allegiance language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=1062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:42</a> | Does the James ossuary's soil prove it came from the Talpiyot tomb? | He says no; the soil argument is too weak to establish that provenance. | He argues that the relevant limestone and iron-rich soils are spread broadly across Jerusalem and the Judean foothills, so a soil match cannot isolate one tomb. The ossuary could have come from several known tombs, an undiscovered tomb, or a robbed tomb, and he adds that politically sensitive burials in Jerusalem make direct archaeological confirmation difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=1378" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:58</a> | Was Moses's Torah later translated or updated because classical Biblical Hebrew is later than Moses? | He says the language history before about 900 BCE is hard to reconstruct, so strong claims here go beyond the evidence. | He says the Torah's spelling and grammar were likely updated, since Hebrew changed over time, but the pre-900 BCE linguistic situation is difficult because we lack enough early Hebrew texts. He thinks some distinct Late Bronze Age Hebrew dialect probably existed, partly because of cultural interchange with Late Egyptian, but he treats the details as conjectural until better evidence appears. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=1650" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:30</a> | How harshly did the Persians and Greeks treat the Egyptians, and could that fit Ezekiel 29? | He says the Persians were especially harsh, the Greeks comparatively better, and the Persian period helps explain Egypt's reduced status. | He describes Persian rule as brutally repressive, including mass killings, and says Alexander was therefore received as a liberator. He connects the Persian periods with Ezekiel 29's "lowly kingdom" theme because Egypt never again raised a native ruler afterward, while Greek rule produced tensions and corruption but was comparatively less oppressive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=2025" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:45</a> | Could Moses have written Genesis at Sinai with God simply revealing the past to him? | He says it is possible but not the most likely model; he prefers a more gradual composition rooted in Moses's background and sources. | He distinguishes possibility from probability: Genesis could have been revealed that way, but he thinks Moses more likely composed much of it progressively during the wilderness period. He points to Egyptian vocabulary and customs as fitting Moses's background, and frames Genesis as an origin account for Israel's mixed multitude that engages surrounding myths as anti-myth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=2236" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:16</a> | Is God's name better rendered `Yahweh` or `Yahuwah`, and is `Yahweh` pagan? | He rejects the pagan claim and prefers `Yahweh`, appealing to Semitic phonology and comparative evidence. | He says the origin of the tetragrammaton is uncertain, including whether the Egyptian reference to the Shasu of Yahu is a place or divine name, so calling `Yahweh` pagan goes beyond proof. He prefers `Yahweh` to `Yahuwah` because Akkadian phonetics and Semitic consonantal patterns support that vocalization more strongly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=2454" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:54</a> | Can you explain the Genesis 1 cosmic temple theory again? | He presents Genesis 1 as temple-oriented creation language, especially in the imaging of humanity and the ordered sacred space motif. | He explains the theory as reading creation through temple categories: God forms ordered spaces, fills them, and creates humanity as his image in cultic or idol-like language. He also links Eden to holy-of-holies imagery, with cherubim and the flaming sword functioning as temple guardians and markers of sacred purity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=2734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:34</a> | Did Egypt ever occupy or vassalize Libya or areas farther west like Canaan? | Egypt did not vassalize Libya as it did Canaan or Kush; it mainly guarded the western border with fortresses and garrisons. | He says Egypt vassalized Canaan and Kush for economic reasons, especially tribute, but Libya lacked goods Egypt wanted enough to justify the same control. Instead, Egypt built western boundary fortresses, such as the Ramesside fortress at El Alamein, and stationed chariot garrisons to watch the Libyan threat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=3005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:05</a> | Could Socrates' daemon or guiding voice play into the New Testament idea of demons? | Yes, the Greek daemon concept may have influenced terminology, but he frames demons as unclean spirits acting outside their intended order. | He says the Greek `daemon` concept is related to the New Testament term and could have influenced the language because Socratic philosophy was widespread. Still, he connects the biblical category more closely with unclean spirits: spirits that abandon their assigned function in God's order and become disruptive or twisted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=3219" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:39</a> | Have you ever studied Old Testament prophecies? | Yes; they are complex because of poetry, recontextualization, and possible double meanings. | He says Old Testament prophecy is difficult because it is usually poetic, compressed language rather than straightforward prose. He also stresses that later generations recontextualize prophecy and that some prophetic texts use amphibole, carrying more than one meaning at once, so interpretation requires care. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=3438" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:18</a> | What are your thoughts on Dr. Schoch's erosion theory about the Sphinx? | He rejects the old-Sphinx water-erosion argument and says Giza wind erosion explains the evidence better. | He says the 10,000-year-old Sphinx theory fails because it does not adequately account for severe wind erosion on the Giza plateau. He compares the Sphinx with the mortuary temple of Menkaure, where known Old Kingdom stonework is heavily sandblasted by wind, and notes that Sphinx conservation problems were already recognized in antiquity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=3691" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:31</a> | Did mankind once worship God and then fall away because of idols? | Personally yes; he thinks polytheism can develop by aggregating local worship traditions, though early evidence is limited. | He personally thinks humanity may once have worshiped one God, but he acknowledges that evidence becomes thin in prehistory. He rejects the old history-of-religions progression from animism to polytheism to monotheism, arguing instead that polytheism can build up when local groups combine their originally local or single-god traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=3932" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:32</a> | What is your producer's favorite color? | The producer likes blue; he likes red. | He answers that his producer's favorite color is blue, while his own is red, and jokes that they have survived many years of marriage despite different color preferences. He also notes that the question seems to be the first time someone asked his producer's favorite color on the channel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=4030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:10</a> | Do early Exodus proponents have to disregard archaeological evidence? | Yes; he says they must ignore or reinterpret evidence such as Pi-Ramesses, Pithom, Merneptah, Hazor, chariots, and Late Egyptian vocabulary. | He says early-date arguments have to work around multiple lines of evidence, including Pi-Ramesses and Pithom, the Merneptah stele, Hazor's Late Bronze destruction, Joseph's chariot context, and Late Egyptian vocabulary in the Pentateuch. He adds that the date of the Exodus should not be treated as a fellowship test, even though some early-date advocates make it one. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=4318" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:58</a> | If Exodus numbers are not literal, what about Gideon's army being reduced to 300? | He connects the 300 to an execration-style ritual and says the army is not presented as doing the fighting itself. | He says the text emphasizes God's power rather than the army's military strength, and he connects the breaking of pots to execration rituals known from the ancient Near East. The 300 may be idealized, but since the men are carrying out a ritual that throws the enemy camp into chaos rather than fighting directly, the number functions differently from a normal battle count. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=4735" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:55</a> | Besides Genesis or Exodus, what Old Testament stories do you find most fascinating historically? | David, Goliath's armor and slingstones, and Hezekiah's interactions with Sennacherib. | He says David is historically fascinating, especially details such as Goliath's Mycenaean-style armor and the archaeological reality of large slingstones. He also mentions Hezekiah's interactions with Sennacherib as another historically rich narrative worth studying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=4948" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:28</a> | Why do many Christians insist the world is 6,000 years old, and what alternatives are preferable? | He does not commit to one origins model; he rejects rigid young-earth chronology and says several alternatives exist. | He says six-thousand-year chronology depends on reading Genesis genealogies as Western-style chronological lists, which he rejects because of honorific transposition and other textual issues. He also dislikes theistic evolution as usually framed, but says alternatives include old-earth creationism, progressive creationism, and some form of gap or resumptive creation theory while he personally withholds commitment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=5471" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:11</a> | What should I keep in mind while reading an older history of Egypt derived from monuments? | Use caution; monuments alone are incomplete, and he suggests reading alongside a newer synthesis such as Ian Shaw. | He has not read the specific older work, but warns that histories of Egypt date quickly because interpretations and discoveries change. Monumental evidence is valuable but incomplete, so he recommends using such a book as a supplement and reading it alongside a broader, newer synthesis such as Ian Shaw's Oxford history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=5670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:30</a> | Are later cuneiform signs influenced by alphabetic letters or Egyptian scripts? | No clear Egyptian-script influence; Ugaritic is alphabetic cuneiform, and possible alphabetic influence would need closer study. | He says Ugaritic is a true alphabet written in cuneiform, but it is hard to prove direct derivation from other alphabetic scripts without examining particular signs. He sees no Egyptian-script influence on cuneiform, though proto-cuneiform had its own pictorial or semi-hieroglyphic look, and he allows that alphabetic and Ugaritic systems may have influenced one another at the phoneme level. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=5971" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:31</a> | What is your favorite secular song? | He names Lacuna Coil's "Comalies" and says he likes symphonic goth. | He says he is a fan of Lacuna Coil and names "Comalies" as one of his favorite secular songs. He describes it as dreamy, interesting symphonic goth and says he likes that style because it has a sense of majesty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=6048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:48</a> | How similar is ancient Egyptian to modern Coptic? | Coptic is the final phase of Egyptian; intelligibility depends on the Egyptian phase, with Coptic sounding slang-like to earlier speakers. | He says Coptic is the final phase of Egyptian, with mostly Egyptian vocabulary and some Greek vocabulary, despite being written in a Greek-like script. Mutual understanding would depend on which Egyptian phase is compared: Middle Egyptian differs greatly from Coptic, but a Middle Kingdom speaker might recognize much of it while finding it slangy, idiomatic, and accented. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=6335" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:35</a> | Is Sumerian the oldest language in the world? | Sumerian and early Egyptian are both around 3100 BCE; which is older is uncertain. | He says the title of oldest written language is debated because early dynastic Egyptian and Sumerian are nearly the same age. Egyptian ivory tags and the oldest Sumerian evidence both cluster around 3100 BCE, so he treats the question as essentially a toss-up rather than a settled point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=6468" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:48</a> | Old earth or young earth, and why? | He does not pick a side; the biblical text can be read more than one way, though common young-earth hermeneutics are problematic. | He says the biblical text is not dogmatic on the earth's age and can be read in more than one way. He thinks a defensible young-earth view would require abandoning common young-earth hermeneutics, which removes the necessity of young-earth conclusions, while old-earth views also have interpretive questions such as how to handle death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=6669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:09</a> | Is flat earth defensible? | No. | He gives an unqualified no, saying flat earth is not defensible in any way or to the slightest degree. The transcript does not develop the point further before he moves to the next super chat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=6699" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:39</a> | How could Manetho connect the Exodus to Thutmose? | He distrusts Manetho for Exodus reconstruction because the preserved versions vary and likely contain later insertions. | He says the preserved versions of Manetho come through Jewish and Christian writers and give differing kings and Exodus stories, which suggests later insertions rather than an original Manetho reference. He still values Manetho for corroborating king lists, but not as a standalone reconstruction of the Exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=7018" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:58</a> | How did ancient nations understand each other's languages? | Through multilingual scribes and diplomatic lingua francas such as Akkadian, later Aramaic and Greek. | He says Egyptians and Hittites did not generally understand each other's languages directly; diplomatic correspondence used Akkadian as a lingua franca. Courts employed multilingual scribes who could draft, translate, and read documents, and later periods shifted the common diplomatic language from Akkadian to Aramaic and then Greek. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=7410" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:30</a> | Do you watch anime? | Yes; he is a longtime anime fan and lists older classics. | He says he loves anime and has watched it since the 1980s, calling himself an older fan of the medium. He lists classics such as Bubblegum Crisis, Akira, Kimagure Orange Road, Maison Ikkoku, Galaxy Express 999, Captain Harlock, and Vampire Princess Miyu, and says he still watches when he has time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=7551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:51</a> | If Hazor was abandoned after its 13th-century destruction, where did Deborah's opponent rule from? | Hazor's monarchy may have survived elsewhere; Judges defeats the army, not necessarily the destroyed city. | He says Hazor itself was not rebuilt for centuries after the 13th-century destruction, but Judges does not describe Deborah besieging Hazor. His solution is that the monarchy or title of "king of Hazor" survived after the city fell, with authority transferred to another citadel, while the narrative defeats Sisera's army rather than Hazor itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=7739" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:59</a> | Are there good Christian Mesopotamian scholars like Kitchen and Hoffmeier are for Egyptology? | He recommends Alan Millard and Paul Lawrence. | He recommends Alan Millard and Paul Lawrence as Christian Mesopotamian scholars comparable in broad role to Kitchen and Hoffmeier for Egyptology. He says he knows both personally, notes that Lawrence studied under Millard, and describes them as balanced, thorough, and excellent scholars. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=7929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:09</a> | Did you watch *Moon Knight* yet? | No, but he expects to watch it for a possible review. | He says he has not watched *Moon Knight* yet. He has been asked to watch it because a friend wants him to do a livestream reviewing the content, so he expects he will need to watch it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=7957" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:37</a> | What study Bible would you recommend? | Study Bibles vary; CSB and ESV have value, but he recommends comparing copies for fit. | He says study Bibles vary widely, so the best choice depends on whether the reader wants translation, commentary, or other helps. He has positive comments about the CSB and ESV study Bibles but also mentions quibbles, especially around Exodus timelines, and recommends comparing physical copies to see what fits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=8266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:46</a> | What is going on with the altar laws in Exodus 20:22-26? | He reads them as separating Yahweh worship from surrounding pagan altar practices, especially cut stones and elevated approaches. | He reads the altar laws in their ancient Near Eastern ritual context, noting that sites such as Bethel, Karnak, and Tel Dan show cut stones, ramps, or stairs that Exodus forbids. He suggests the laws distinguish Yahweh worship from surrounding practices, preserve ritual purity, and prefer natural stone at least for certain altar types, while acknowledging later Israelite practice was not uniform. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=8679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:39</a> | Should I worry that the ESV has a complementarian bias? | He is not worried about the translation itself, though he is more cautious about commentary notes. | He says he does not consider the ESV translation itself something to fear and calls it a good translation, though not his favorite. He is more concerned about ideology entering translations or study Bible notes, so he would watch commentary notes more carefully than the ESV text itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=8802" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:42</a> | Have you ever been to the Valley of Whales in Egypt? | No. | He says he has been all over Egypt but has not visited the Valley of Whales. He likes fossils and ancient remains, but says he usually does not go somewhere in Egypt unless it has Egyptian temples. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=8850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:30</a> | How many languages do you know? | He says he has reading competency in 13 and is working on a 14th. | He clarifies that by "knowing" a language he means reading competency, not speaking ability. He says he reads 13 languages and is working on a 14th, with Ukrainian as a spoken-language beginner project at that time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=9048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:48</a> | What convinced you out of young-earth creationism? | Modern hermeneutics and reading the Bible in its ancient context. | He says the decisive issue was hermeneutics rather than science: once he began reading the Bible in its ancient context, he could no longer get young-earth creationism from the text. He adds that the scientific problems are connected to that interpretive problem, but the root for him was contextual biblical interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/l80zyabM1gE?t=9253" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:13</a> | Can you talk more about the "four loves in Greek" idea? | He argues the common sermon version is badly overstated; the Greek love words overlap far more than that popular teaching admits. | He says the popular sermon distinction between erotic, brotherly, and divine love is mostly wrong because `eros`, `phileo`, and `agapao` can each function as ordinary words for love. He explains that later usage shifts, such as `phileo` developing associations with kissing, help explain why another term could be preferred, but the words do not map neatly onto the popular four-love categories. |
